summary:
After getting new full dentures, the person struggles to swallow because there may be too much space left above their teeth when they bite down, making it hard for food to pass smoothly into the throat without obstruction from poorly fitting or positioned false teeth. This issue often stems from an incorrect setting during dental work which affects how well everything lines up vertically inside one’s mouth while eating. Agent1 reasoned based primarily around improper alignment caused by overly high upper jaw prosthetics leading to difficulties moving solid foods through properly; hence selecting 'excess vertical dimension' as primary cause among provided choices makes logical sense clinically speaking regarding common post-dentistry complications patients face immediately thereafter concerning basic functions such as chewing & gulping sustenance safely/efficiently. 

The summary captures two main ideas succinctly focusing specifically upon critical aspects pertinent towards addressing core query posed initially – namely nature underlying medical condition affecting ability to ingest normally coupled alongside explanation tying back specific anatomical/mechanistic factors implicated therein rooted firmly within context outlined originally via problem statement presented at outset here today!
